g.data-narration: Welcome to Inter-Process Communication. We explore how processes cooperate and exchange data in operating systems.
Processes can be independent (working alone) or cooperating (sharing data). Independent processes cannot affect each other.
Processes cooperate for four key reasons: Information sharing, Computation speedup, Modularity, and Convenience (ICMC).
Two fundamental IPC models exist: Shared Memory (fast, direct access) and Message Passing (via OS kernel).
In Shared Memory, processes read and write directly to a common memory region. OS sets up the region initially.
The Producer-Consumer problem is classic IPC: producer makes items, consumer uses them, with a buffer in between.
Bounded buffer uses circular array with 'in' (producer writes here) and 'out' (consumer reads here) pointers.
Buffer is FULL when (in+1) % N equals out. Buffer is EMPTY when in equals out. One slot is wasted to distinguish states.
Message Passing uses send() and receive() operations. The kernel manages message queues between processes.
Direct Communication explicitly names the recipient (send(P, msg)) or sender (receive(Q, msg)). Links auto-establish between pairs.
g.text: Link: exactly 2 processes, 1 link per pair

g.data-narration: Synchronization can be blocking (sender waits) or non-blocking (sender continues). Both blocking = rendezvous.
Message queues have three capacities: Zero (rendezvous), Bounded (sender blocks when full), Unbounded (never blocks).
Shared Memory is faster but harder to program. Message Passing is slower but easier and better for distributed systems.
Key exam points: Draw diagrams, memorize bounded buffer code, know full/empty conditions, ICMC reasons, and comparisons.
We covered IPC fundamentals: Independent vs Cooperating processes, Shared Memory and Message Passing models, and the Producer-Consumer paradigm.
g.text: Independent processes work alone | Cooperating processes share data and require IPC

g.data-narration: Shared Memory offers fast direct access but requires careful synchronization. Message Passing is easier but slower. Choose based on your use case.
g.text: Use Shared Memory for speed & large data | Use Message Passing for modularity & distributed systems

g.data-narration: Direct communication names processes explicitly. Indirect uses mailboxes for better modularity. Synchronization types determine blocking behavior.
g.text: Direct: explicit naming | Indirect: via mailboxes | Blocking: waits | Non-blocking: continues | Both blocking = rendezvous

g.data-narration: Thank you for learning about Inter-Process Communication. Practice with diagrams and code to master this essential OS concept.
